title: Detecting Use-After-Free (CWE-416)
description: Learn how static analysis detects use-after-free vulnerabilities using CFG, value-flow, and points-to analysis.

steps.title: The Vulnerable Program
steps.text: A string is duplicated via malloc, then freed, but accessed again afterward. This is a use-after-free vulnerability (CWE-416). After free(copy), the code reads copy[0] — a dangling pointer access that can lead to crashes or exploitation.

steps.title: Control Flow
steps.text: The CFG shows the linear flow through process(). After free(copy), execution continues to copy[0] — the dangling pointer access. There's no branch that could skip the buggy line.

steps.title: Value Flow
steps.text: The value-flow graph traces data from malloc to buf to the return value to copy. After free(copy), the value still flows to copy[0]. This malloc-free-use chain is the signature of a UAF.

steps.title: Points-To Analysis
steps.text: PTA reveals that both copy and buf point to the same malloc allocation. When free(copy) executes, the allocation is deallocated, but copy still holds a dangling pointer.

steps.title: Finding the Bug
steps.text: By combining value-flow (data reaches freed memory) with PTA (copy aliases buf), we can confirm the use-after-free. The path malloc to free to use is the detection pattern.
steps.prompt: Try removing the free(copy) line and re-analyzing — the value-flow chain to copy[0] should look different.
